title: Coincidence Detection Is All You Need [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
## **Coincidence Detection Is All You Need**

## **Anonymous Author(s)**

Affiliation Address `email`

## **Abstract**

1 This paper demonstrates that the performance of coincidence detection - a classic 2 neuromorphic signal processing method found in Rosenblatt’s perceptrons with 3 distributed transmission times, can be competitive to a state-of-the-art deep learning 4 method for pattern recognition. Hence, we cannot remain comfortably numb to the 5 prevailing dogma that efficient matrix-vector operations is all we need; but should 6 enquire with greater vigour if more advanced continual learning methods (running 7 on spiking neural network hardware with neuromodulatory mechanisms at multiple 8 timescales) can beat the accuracy of task-specific deep learning methods.

## 9 **1 Introduction**

- 10 Frank Rosenblatt and his team (1957-1971) built and analyzed several kinds of perceptrons [1, 2, 3, 4] 11 - networks of sensory, association and receptor neurons; which in contemporary deep learning termi12 nology relates to the input, hidden and output layers. The propagating signals were binary (compatible 13 with a spike-based view), the synaptic delays (transmission times) and weights (memory states) could 14 be analog, the network could be recurrent and was often randomly interconnected, and learning 15 often meant tuning the weights of the association-receptor subnetwork by some error-corrective 16 reinforcement. The synaptic delays were not learnt but instead randomly distributed in Rosenblatt’s 17 Tobermory perceptrons [5], and this was rich enough to realize concentration-invariant and uniform 18 time-warp invariant spatiotemporal classification by logarithmic encoding and coincidence detection. 19 However, the processing speed of commercial Von Neumann computers advanced exponentially 20 and outperformed neuromorphic hardware on yesterdecade’s benchmarks [6]. The Tobermory per21 ceptron was forgotten, nevertheless, the utility of logarithmic encoding and coincidence detection 22 was formalized by John Hopfield [7] as an efficient solution to the _analog match_ problem in pattern 23 recognition.

- 24 Now, half a century after the accidental demise of Rosenblatt, neuromorphic signal processors are 25 making a comeback. For example, (1) Intel’s Loihi with spike-time dependent plasticity mechanisms 26 for learning olfactory pattern recognizers [8]; (2) Physical reservoir computing networks [9] where 27 the interconnectivity of the hidden layer is unchanged, closer to the spirit of Rosenblatt’s randomly 28 interconnected sensory-association subnetwork.

- 29 Here, to strengthen the case for revisiting classic methods on novel and modern hardware, we evaluate 30 the performance of coincidence detection in comparison to a deep learning method. Nothing more, 31 nothing less, although this work was triggered by a rabid interest in employing artificial intelligence 32 to sniff out infections and prevent future pandemics.
Submitted to 36th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2022). Do not distribute.

Table 1: Test accuracy (%)

|ResNet-26|Coincidence detection|
|---|---|
|82.2_±_0.3 (from [10])|82.7 (this work)|

## 33 **2 Methods**

- 34 Here, we consider the work [10] of an interdisciplinary team, where a 26 layer convolutional neural 35 network with residual connections (ResNet-26) was successfully trained for classifying pathogenic 36 bacteria by Raman spectroscopy. In their work, there are _N_ = 30 classes of bacterial isolates and 37 they begin with a ResNet-26 pre-trained on _N ×_ 2000 spectra, then for each class _n_ = 1 : _N_ there are 38 _M_ = 100 training spectra, and similarly _N × M_ = 3000 test spectra. Each spectrum _**x**_ contains 1000 39 floating-point numbers ranging between 0 and 1. Although compute intensive, their deep learning 40 method proved to be a tool of great convenience for pattern recognition in a challenging dataset, 41 where intra-isolate spectra were often more dissimilar than inter-isolate spectra.

- 42 Our method to tackle the above dataset, is inspired by the theory of how coincidence detection [7] 43 in animal brains is fundamental for odour classification in complex and turbulent mixtures. Each 44 class _n_ has a vector representation _**w** n_ that is learnt, and an input vector _**x**_ results in an output 45 class _y_ ( _**x**_ ) = arg _n_ max( _**x**_[�] _**w** n_ ) where we introduce the operator[�] to represent the coincidence 46 between two signals. The analytical nature of coincidence detection depends on the specificities of the 47 ion-channels and the membranes involved [11], and may even incorporate nonlinear leaky-integrate 48 [12] multiple timescale mechanisms. We do not yet have a complete theory of neuromorphic signal 49 processing, so here we introduce an approximation for the translation and scale-invariant property of 50 coincidence detection as

- 50

- 51 where _**x**_ ˆ is the zero-mean unit-variance normalization of _**x**_ .

- 52 Thus, the approximation in Eq. (1) allows _y_ ( _**x**_ ) to be learnt by a logistic regression on the normalized 53 dataset. We discard the pre-training data, pre-process the training and test spectra by a range-1 mean 54 filter, and use the default method for logistic regression in Wolfram Mathematica (L2-regularization 55 = 0.0001, optimization method = limited-memory BFGS). Code is provided in the supplemental 56 material for reproducibility.

## 57

## **3 Result and outlook**

- 58 The coincidence detection (via normalized logistic regression) method introduced here achieves a test 59 accuracy greater than ResNet-26 (see Table 1), and it took less than 3 seconds to train the classifier 60 on a modern desktop (without any special-purpose GPUs). Check the Appendix for a confusion 61 matrix plot of the training and test data. Note that the training data was fit all at once to a 100% 62 accuracy. With a more neuromorphic coincidence detection method and a learning method that adapts 63 the synaptic delays _**w**_ continually, to keep track under changing environmental conditions, we may 64 achieve even greater accuracies.

## 65

## **References**

- 66 [1] Frank Rosenblatt. _The perceptron, a perceiving and recognizing automaton Project Para_ . 67 Cornell Aeronautical Laboratory, Inc. Report no. 85-460-1, 1957.
- 68 [2] Frank Rosenblatt. _The perceptron: A theory of statistical separability in cognitive systems_ . 69 Cornell Aeronautical Laboratory, Inc. Report no. VG-1196-G-1, 1958.
- 70 [3] Frank Rosenblatt. _Principles of neurodynamics. perceptrons and the theory of brain mechanisms_ . 71 Cornell Aeronautical Laboratory, Inc. Report no. 1196-G-8, 1961.

2

- 72 [4] Frank Rosenblatt. Cognitive systems research program. Technical report, Cornell University, 73 Ithaca, New York, 1971.
- 74 [5] Frank Rosenblatt. A description of the tobermory perceptron. In _Collected Technical Papers_ , 75 volume 2. Cornell University, Ithaca, New York, 1963.
- 76 [6] George Nagy. Neural networks-then and now. _IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks_ , 2(2):316– 77 318, 1991.
- 78 [7] John J Hopfield. Pattern recognition computation using action potential timing for stimulus 79 representation. _Nature_ , 376(6535):33–36, 1995.
- 80 [8] Nabil Imam and Thomas A Cleland. Rapid online learning and robust recall in a neuromorphic 81 olfactory circuit. _Nature Machine Intelligence_ , 2(3):181–191, 2020.

- 82 [9] G. Tanaka, T. Yamane, J.B. Héroux, R. Nakane, N. Kanazawa, S. Takeda, H. Numata, D. Nakano, 83 and A. Hirose. Recent advances in physical reservoir computing: A review. _Neural Networks_ , 84 115:100–123, 2019.

- 85 [10] Chi-Sing Ho, Neal Jean, Catherine A Hogan, Lena Blackmon, Stefanie S Jeffrey, Mark Holodniy, 86 Niaz Banaei, Amr AE Saleh, Stefano Ermon, and Jennifer Dionne. Rapid identification of 87 pathogenic bacteria using raman spectroscopy and deep learning. _Nature communications_ , 88 10(1):1–8, 2019.
- 89 [11] Nelson Spruston. Pyramidal neurons: dendritic structure and synaptic integration. _Nature_ 90 _Reviews Neuroscience_ , 9(3):206–221, 2008.

- 91 [12] Wondimu Teka, Toma M Marinov, and Fidel Santamaria. Neuronal spike timing adapta92 tion described with a fractional leaky integrate-and-fire model. _PLoS computational biology_ , 93 10(3):e1003526, 2014.

## 94 **Checklist**

- 95 1. For all authors...

- 96 (a) Do the main claims made in the abstract and introduction accurately reflect the paper’s 97 contributions and scope? [Yes] See Table 1.
- 98 (b) Did you describe the limitations of your work? [Yes] Equation (1) makes it clear that 99 we employ an approximation for coincidence detection.

- 100 (c) Did you discuss any potential negative societal impacts of your work? [N/A]

- 101 (d) Have you read the ethics review guidelines and ensured that your paper conforms to 102 them? [Yes]

- 103 2. If you are including theoretical results...

- 104 (a) Did you state the full set of assumptions of all theoretical results? [N/A]

- 105 (b) Did you include complete proofs of all theoretical results? [N/A]

- 106 3. If you ran experiments...

- 107 (a) Did you include the code, data, and instructions needed to reproduce the main ex108 perimental results (either in the supplemental material or as a URL)? [Yes] Check 109 supplemental material
- 110 (b) Did you specify all the training details (e.g., data splits, hyperparameters, how they 111 were chosen)? [Yes]
- 112 (c) Did you report error bars (e.g., with respect to the random seed after running experi113 ments multiple times)? [N/A]
- 114 (d) Did you include the total amount of compute and the type of resources used (e.g., type 115 of GPUs, internal cluster, or cloud provider)? [Yes] qualitatively, in the results section
- 116 4. If you are using existing assets (e.g., code, data, models) or curating/releasing new assets...

- 117 (a) If your work uses existing assets, did you cite the creators? [Yes]

3

118

- (b) Did you mention the license of the assets? [Yes] In the supplemental information

- 119 (c) Did you include any new assets either in the supplemental material or as a URL? [No]

- 120 (d) Did you discuss whether and how consent was obtained from people whose data you’re 121 using/curating? [N/A]
- 122 (e) Did you discuss whether the data you are using/curating contains personally identifiable 123 information or offensive content? [N/A]

- 124 5. If you used crowdsourcing or conducted research with human subjects...

- 125 (a) Did you include the full text of instructions given to participants and screenshots, if 126 applicable? [N/A]
- 127 (b) Did you describe any potential participant risks, with links to Institutional Review 128 Board (IRB) approvals, if applicable? [N/A]
- 129 (c) Did you include the estimated hourly wage paid to participants and the total amount 130 spent on participant compensation? [N/A]

131

## **A Appendix**

> 132 Confusion matrix of the training and test data. Wolfram Mathematica code to reproduce these results 133 is provided as supplemental material.
This document was extracted by OCR engine and could contain mistakes.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Progressive (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: This paper presents a provocative and timely re-examination of classical neuromorphic principles—specifically coincidence detection in the spirit of Rosenblatt’s early perceptrons—and positions them as competitive alternatives to modern deep learning architectures for pattern recognition. The core claim, that a simple normalized logistic regression model inspired by biological coincidence detection can match and even slightly exceed the performance of a ResNet-26 on a challenging Raman spectroscopy dataset for bacterial classification, is striking and potentially impactful. The clarity of the narrative is generally strong, with a well-motivated introduction that effectively contextualizes historical and contemporary neuromorphic computing trends. However, several high-level concerns significantly affect the paper's scientific rigor and persuasiveness. Most critically, the methodological description is incomplete: Equation (1), which defines the central mechanism of coincidence detection, is entirely missing, undermining the reproducibility and technical credibility of the approach. Additionally, while the reported accuracy (82.7% vs. 82.2%) appears favorable, the lack of statistical testing, confidence intervals for the coincidence detection result, or multiple runs to assess variance raises questions about the robustness of the comparison. The claim of superior performance rests on a single data point, in contrast to the ResNet-26 result cited from prior work with a standard deviation reported. The paper also oversimplifies the equivalence between coincidence detection and logistic regression on normalized data without sufficient biological or computational justification, potentially misrepresenting the neuromorphic premise. Finally, the absence of computational cost or inference-time comparisons limits the practical implications of the “efficiency” argument. While the vision of reviving classical ideas for modern neuromorphic hardware is compelling and aligns with growing interest in energy-efficient, adaptive AI, the current submission requires substantial methodological clarification, rigorous experimental validation, and more careful framing of its claims to support its ambitious title.

comments.title: Unspecified and potentially erroneous coincidence detection operator with missing formal definition and equation
comments.quote: _y_ ( _**x**_ ) = arg _n_ max( _**x**_[] _**w** n_ ) where we introduce the operator[] to represent the coincidence between two signals.
comments.explanation: The operator '[]' is introduced to represent 'coincidence between two signals' but appears corrupted due to likely OCR failure (e.g., missing asterisk or convolution symbol). Although the intended notation may be '[∗]' as suggested in supplementary material, the operator is never formally defined, and Equation (1), which should define the approximation, is missing from the text. This omission is critical because the entire method hinges on this operator to achieve translation and scale invariance via neuromorphic coincidence detection. The claim that the method reduces to logistic regression on normalized data lacks a logical bridge—no derivation shows how the coincidence operator approximates an inner product in normalized space. Even if the code resolves this, the paper fails to define its core operation, undermining reproducibility and technical rigor.

comments.title: Test accuracy comparison lacks statistical rigor, error quantification, and controlled experimental setup
comments.quote:
|ResNet-26|Coincidence detection|
|---|---|
|82.2±0.3 (from [10])|82.7 (this work)|
comments.explanation: The paper claims superiority over ResNet-26 based on a 0.5% higher test accuracy (82.7% vs. 82.2% ± 0.3%) but provides no error bars or uncertainty estimates for its own result. It is unclear whether 82.7% reflects a single run or average over multiple trials. Given the small margin, the lack of statistical reporting makes it impossible to assess significance. Furthermore, the experimental conditions differ: the authors use only 100 samples per class without pre-training, while ResNet-26 results from [10] are based on 2000 spectra per class with pre-training. While this may highlight sample efficiency, the disparity must be acknowledged and contextualized. Without proper uncertainty quantification or a matched evaluation protocol, the claim of outperforming ResNet-26 is misleading.

comments.title: Ambiguous and inconsistent reporting of training accuracy, confusion matrix, and data splits undermines central results
comments.quote: Note that the training data was fit all at once to a 100% accuracy.
comments.explanation: The authors claim 100% training accuracy, suggesting perfect memorization, yet report only 82.7% test accuracy. With L2-regularization at 0.0001, such a fit raises concerns about overfitting, particularly given the model complexity and number of classes. This concern is amplified by the confusion matrix in the appendix, which is presented as OCR-derived text and shows numerical inconsistencies: the 'actual class' row sums to 3032 test samples, exceeding the stated 3000 (30 classes × 100 spectra). Values like 144 and 130 suggest >100 predictions for some actual classes, contradicting the data split description. Without a clearly labeled, verifiable confusion matrix, the validity of both training and test accuracy claims is in doubt. This ambiguity critically weakens confidence in the reported performance.

comments.title: Conceptual confusion between synaptic weights and synaptic delays undermines neuromorphic plausibility
comments.quote: adapts the synaptic delays _**w**_ continually
comments.explanation: The symbol _**w**_ is consistently used as a synaptic weight (memory state), particularly in the classifier formulation. However, in a future outlook, the authors refer to adapting 'synaptic delays _**w**_'—a fundamental conflation, as synaptic delays (transmission times) and weights (strengths) are distinct physical quantities in neuromorphic systems. Though this occurs in a speculative context, misusing established neuromorphic terminology risks misleading readers about the biological plausibility of the proposed extensions. Given the paper's emphasis on neuromorphic principles, maintaining conceptual clarity between these parameters is essential.

comments.title: Ambiguity in normalization and preprocessing procedures risks reproducibility and data leakage
comments.quote: pre-process the training and test spectra by a range-1 mean filter
comments.explanation: Two key preprocessing steps are ambiguously described. First, 'range-1 mean filter' is non-standard and undefined—it may mean a moving average with ±1 window, but kernel design, causality, and boundary handling are unspecified. Second, although zero-mean unit-variance normalization is applied, it is not stated whether normalization parameters (mean, std) are computed solely on training data or on the full dataset. The latter would introduce test-time data leakage, inflating performance. While common practice avoids leakage, the omission in the main text risks misinterpretation, especially for less experienced readers. These gaps affect reproducibility despite supplemental code.

comments.title: Misrepresentation of hyperparameter settings as 'default' undermines methodological transparency
comments.quote: use the default method for logistic regression in Wolfram Mathematica (L2-regularization = 0.0001, optimization method = limited-memory BFGS)
comments.explanation: The authors describe their hyperparameters as the 'default method' in Mathematica, but L2-regularization = 0.0001 is not typical of default settings in Wolfram's Classify or LogisticRegression functions, where regularization is often disabled or controlled differently. This mischaracterization implies the model is minimally tuned when in fact a specific regularization value was chosen—potentially influencing performance in a high-stakes comparison. While the value is correctly reported, labeling it as 'default' misrepresents the experimental design and could lead to failed replication attempts if users rely on actual defaults.

progressive_original__qwen3-235b-a22b-25.label: Progressive Original (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive_original__qwen3-235b-a22b-25.overall_feedback: This paper presents a provocative and timely re-examination of classical neuromorphic principles—specifically coincidence detection in the spirit of Rosenblatt’s early perceptrons—and positions them as competitive alternatives to modern deep learning architectures for pattern recognition. The core claim, that a simple normalized logistic regression model inspired by biological coincidence detection can match and even slightly exceed the performance of a ResNet-26 on a challenging Raman spectroscopy dataset for bacterial classification, is striking and potentially impactful. The clarity of the narrative is generally strong, with a well-motivated introduction that effectively contextualizes historical and contemporary neuromorphic computing trends. However, several high-level concerns significantly affect the paper's scientific rigor and persuasiveness. Most critically, the methodological description is incomplete: Equation (1), which defines the central mechanism of coincidence detection, is entirely missing, undermining the reproducibility and technical credibility of the approach. Additionally, while the reported accuracy (82.7% vs. 82.2%) appears favorable, the lack of statistical testing, confidence intervals for the coincidence detection result, or multiple runs to assess variance raises questions about the robustness of the comparison. The claim of superior performance rests on a single data point, in contrast to the ResNet-26 result cited from prior work with a standard deviation reported. The paper also oversimplifies the equivalence between coincidence detection and logistic regression on normalized data without sufficient biological or computational justification, potentially misrepresenting the neuromorphic premise. Finally, the absence of computational cost or inference-time comparisons limits the practical implications of the “efficiency” argument. While the vision of reviving classical ideas for modern neuromorphic hardware is compelling and aligns with growing interest in energy-efficient, adaptive AI, the current submission requires substantial methodological clarification, rigorous experimental validation, and more careful framing of its claims to support its ambitious title.

comments.title: Unspecified and potentially erroneous coincidence detection operator
comments.quote: _y_ ( _**x**_ ) = arg _n_ max( _**x**_[] _**w** n_ ) where we introduce the operator[] to represent the coincidence between two signals.
comments.explanation: The operator [] is introduced as representing 'coincidence between two signals', but the symbol '' appears to be a corrupted OCR result (likely a missing or misread glyph). Given the context, it may have been intended to be a dot product, a convolution, or a more complex neuromorphic operation as referenced in Hopfield [7]. However, even assuming the intended symbol was correctly rendered (e.g., a dot or star), the operator is never formally defined, and Equation (1), which follows, is missing in the text. This omission is critical because the entire method hinges on this approximation of coincidence detection. While the authors later state that the method reduces to logistic regression on normalized data, the logical bridge—how the operator [] leads to or approximates a standard inner product in a normalized space—is not shown. The claim that this yields translation and scale invariance is plausible in certain neuromorphic models, but without a clear definition of the operator or its approximation, the method is underspecified. The supplemental code might resolve this, but the paper itself fails to define the core operation, undermining reproducibility and technical rigor.

comments.title: Missing equation (Equation 1) critical to method definition
comments.quote:
so here we introduce an approximation for the translation and scale-invariant property of coincidence detection as

- 50

- 51 where _**x**_ ˆ is the zero-mean unit-variance normalization of _**x**_ .
comments.explanation: The passage indicates that an approximation is introduced 'as' followed by a blank (lines 50–51), and then refers to 'Eq. (1)' on line 52. This missing equation is essential because it is supposed to formalize the coincidence detection approximation that enables logistic regression. Without it, we cannot verify whether the stated approach correctly models translation and scale invariance, nor can we confirm the connection between coincidence detection and logistic regression. While the authors correctly state that zero-mean unit-variance normalization is applied and that logistic regression is used, the justification for why this normalization approximates neuromorphic coincidence detection is unsubstantiated without the equation. This gap suggests either a severe formatting error from OCR or a missing derivation. Even if the formula is in the supplemental code, the paper's standalone readability and scientific completeness are compromised.

comments.title: Inconsistent treatment of training data and potential overfitting not addressed
comments.quote: Note that the training data was fit all at once to a 100% accuracy.
comments.explanation: The authors state that the training data was fitted to 100% accuracy, which suggests perfect memorization. While logistic regression can achieve high accuracy on well-separated data, perfect fit on 30 classes with 100 samples each (3000 training samples) is unusual and raises concerns about overfitting, especially given the test accuracy is only 82.7%. The paper does not explain how such perfect training fit aligns with the reported test performance. One would expect a larger gap or signs of regularization failure, but the L2-regularization is set to a small value (0.0001), which is standard but may not be sufficient to prevent overfitting with perfect separation. The confusion matrix in the appendix appears to show counts per class (all 100s on diagonal for training?), but the second block shows non-100 values, possibly indicating test errors. However, without proper labeling, the matrix is ambiguous. The claim of 100% training accuracy combined with a missing equation and vague operator definition weakens confidence in the validity of the comparison to ResNet-26, which reports an error bar (±0.3%), while the coincidence detection result gives no uncertainty estimate.

comments.title: Test accuracy comparison lacks statistical rigor and reproducibility details
comments.quote:
|ResNet-26|Coincidence detection|
|---|---|
|82.2_±_0.3 (from [10])|82.7 (this work)|
comments.explanation: The paper compares its method's test accuracy (82.7%, no error bar) against ResNet-26's 82.2% ± 0.3% from [10]. This comparison is problematic for several reasons. First, the authors do not indicate whether their 82.7% is an average over multiple runs or a single trial, nor do they report variance, which is essential for claiming superiority. Given the small margin (0.5%), the lack of error bars makes it impossible to assess statistical significance. In contrast, [10] reports a standard error, suggesting multiple trials. Second, the experimental setup differs: the authors discard pre-training data and use only 100 samples per class, while [10] uses pre-training on 2000 spectra per class. This discrepancy in data usage undermines the fairness of the comparison. Although this may be intentional to highlight sample efficiency, it should be explicitly acknowledged and contextualized. The claim of 'greater than ResNet-26' accuracy is thus misleading without uncertainty quantification or a controlled setup.

comments.title: Mislabeling of synaptic delays and weight vectors
comments.quote: adapts the synaptic delays _**w**_ continually
comments.explanation: The paper consistently refers to _**w** n_ as the vector representation of class n and uses it in the context of learning (e.g., via logistic regression), which implies it represents synaptic weights. However, on line 63, the authors say: 'a learning method that adapts the synaptic delays _**w**_ continually'. This is a clear notational inconsistency: _**w**_ was introduced as a weight (memory state) in line 13 and used as such in the classifier, but here it is called a 'synaptic delay'. Synaptic delays and weights are distinct physical and computational quantities in neuromorphic systems (as earlier noted in line 13: 'synaptic delays (transmission times) and weights (memory states) could be analog'). Conflating them suggests a fundamental conceptual confusion. While this occurs in a speculative future outlook, misusing established terminology in a technical domain can mislead readers about the nature of the proposed extension. It may be a simple slip, but given the paper's focus on biological plausibility, such a distinction matters.

comments.title: Ambiguity in data preprocessing steps
comments.quote: pre-process the training and test spectra by a range-1 mean filter
comments.explanation: The term 'range-1 mean filter' is not standard and is not defined. It might refer to a moving average filter with window size 3 (centered on each point, spanning ±1), but this is unclear. The lack of specification affects reproducibility: without knowing the exact filtering operation (e.g., kernel size, causality, boundary handling), it is difficult to replicate the results. While the code is provided in the supplement, the paper itself should describe key preprocessing steps. This is especially important because filtering can affect classification performance, particularly in spectral data like Raman signals. The omission is minor if the code is complete, but it still violates best practices for method reporting in scientific papers.

comments.title: Ambiguity in confusion matrix interpretation undermines reported accuracy claim
comments.explanation: The appendix presents a confusion matrix as image-derived text, but its structure is ambiguous. The first block appears to list predicted class counts (all 100s), followed by another block (also all 100s), then a row labeled 'actual class' with values summing to 3032 — which exceeds the expected 3000 test samples (N×M = 30×100). This suggests either OCR corruption or a mislabeled/incorrect matrix. The stated 82.7% accuracy implies ~2481 correct predictions, but summing the diagonal of the final row (97 + 103 + ... + 113) gives a total of 3032, which is inconsistent. If this row is the sum of predictions per actual class, then some classes have more than 100 test samples — contradicting Section 2's statement that there are M=100 test spectra per class. The authors claim perfect training fit and 82.7% test accuracy, but without a correct confusion matrix, this result cannot be verified. Even assuming OCR errors (e.g., '103' instead of '100'), the presence of values like 144 and 130 strongly suggests over 100 predictions for single classes, which would indicate a data split error. While the checklist claims reproducibility, this figure — critical for validation — is illegible and numerically inconsistent. This undermines confidence in the central accuracy claim.

comments.title: Inconsistent hyperparameter reporting undermines reproducibility
comments.quote: use the default method for logistic regression in Wolfram Mathematica (L2-regularization = 0.0001, optimization method = limited-memory BFGS)
comments.explanation: The authors state they used L2-regularization = 0.0001 and L-BFGS, but describe this as the 'default method' in Mathematica. However, Wolfram Mathematica's default logistic regression settings do not typically expose or fix L2-regularization to such a specific small value; regularization is often off by default or controlled via different parameters (e.g., 'L2Regularization' in Classify). The specificity of 0.0001 suggests a deliberate choice, not a default. This mischaracterization risks misleading readers attempting reproduction: they may use true defaults and get different results. While the value is provided (addressing part of the concern), labeling it as 'default' incorrectly implies robustness or conventionality where a hyperparameter choice was actually made. Since accuracy differences between 82.2% and 82.7% are small, this choice could be consequential. The authors correctly report the value, but misrepresent its procedural origin.

comments.title: Test accuracy claimed without error bars contradicts standard practice
comments.quote: the coincidence detection (via normalized logistic regression) method introduced here achieves a test accuracy greater than ResNet-26 (see Table 1)
comments.explanation: Table 1 reports ResNet-26 accuracy as 82.2 ± 0.3 (from [10]), indicating standard deviation over runs, but the proposed method reports exactly '82.7' with no uncertainty. Section 3 says the method trains in under 3 seconds, implying deterministic fitting (especially since logistic regression with fixed data and L-BFGS is deterministic in Mathematica). However, without confirmation of determinism, and given the small margin of improvement (0.5%), the lack of error reporting raises concerns. The NeurIPS checklist marks [112] as [N/A], justifying no error bars, but this is questionable — even deterministic models should report if single-run results are due to zero variance. In high-stakes comparisons near statistical parity, omitting uncertainty undermines the claim of superiority. Context resolves part of this: speed implies no multiple seeds were run, but the authors should explicitly state this rather than rely on implication.

comments.title: Potential OCR error in coincidence detection operator notation
comments.quote: arg _n_ max( _**x**_[] _**w** n_ ) where we introduce the operator[] to represent the coincidence
comments.explanation: The symbol '[]' appears to be a corrupted Unicode character, likely an OCR failure where the intended operator (possibly '⋆', '⊛', or a calligraphic character) was lost. In the PAPER SUMMARY, it is correctly rendered as '[∗]', matching the LaTeX $[\ast]$. The surrounding text uses '[∗]' consistently in definitions, and Eq. (1) uses \hat{\mathbf{x}}^\top \mathbf{w}_n, so the intent is clear: define a symbolic coincidence operator approximated by dot product. Since the summary confirms the intended notation and the meaning is recoverable from context, this is not an author error. However, in the main text, '' may confuse a reader. Given OCR warnings, this is best treated as a display issue rather than a substantive flaw.

comments.title: Ambiguity in normalization procedure affects reproducibility of core approximation
comments.quote: and use the default method for logistic regression in Wolfram Mathematica (L2-regularization = 0.0001, optimization method = limited-memory BFGS)
comments.explanation: The method states that spectra are normalized to zero-mean, unit-variance (\hat{\mathbf{x}}), but does not specify whether this normalization is fitted on training data only and then applied to test data, or done globally. Given that the model trains in seconds and claims high accuracy, it is crucial that the normalization procedure does not leak test information. The authors correctly pre-process both training and test spectra, but do not explicitly state whether normalization parameters (mean, std) are estimated solely from training data. While standard practice dictates this, and the checklist claims full training details are provided, the omission in the main text could mislead novice readers into applying global normalization, which would inflate performance. The phrase 'pre-process the training and test spectra' without qualification leaves this ambiguous. Given the importance of proper data leakage prevention in benchmarking, this is a meaningful gap, though likely resolvable from code in supplemental material.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper offers an intriguing, historically grounded perspective on pattern recognition, successfully challenging the "deep learning is all you need" paradigm by demonstrating, through a specific application in Raman spectroscopy, that classic coincidence detection can achieve competitive results with significantly lower computational overhead. The writing is generally clear and motivated by a compelling synthesis of neuromorphic theory and practical utility. However, the manuscript suffers from a significant methodological gap: the transition from the biological/neuromorphic premise of coincidence detection to the practical implementation—a simple logistic regression—is opaque. The paper fails to sufficiently justify why a standard L2-regularized linear model serves as a valid or rigorous "approximation" of complex ion-channel dynamics and spatiotemporal coincidence detection. To improve the paper, the authors must bridge this theoretical disconnect, provide a more granular comparison of the signal processing architectures beyond raw accuracy (e.g., robustness to noise or data scarcity), and justify why the chosen dataset is a fair benchmark for evaluating the specific biological advantages of neuromorphic-inspired architectures.

comments.title: Missing Mathematical Definitions and Operational Clarity
comments.quote: arg $n$ max( $\mathbf{x}$[] $\mathbf{w}_n$ ) / Eq. (1)
comments.explanation: The paper fails to provide Equation (1), which is central to the proposed coincidence detection methodology, and presents the [] operator as an undefined placeholder. These omissions prevent reproducibility and make it impossible to verify the mathematical soundness of the 'approximation' claimed in the text.

comments.title: Inconsistent Evaluation Methodology and Class Imbalance Handling
comments.quote: N = 30; M = 100; accuracy comparison to ResNet-26
comments.explanation: There are significant discrepancies between the stated experimental protocol ($M=100$ spectra per class) and the actual counts shown in the confusion matrix (which reveals highly unbalanced class sizes ranging from 33 to 144). Furthermore, the reported accuracy comparison against the ResNet-26 baseline is underspecified, as the authors do not clarify how class imbalance was addressed or if the feature extraction comparison is equitable given the different handling of pre-training data.

progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.label: Progressive Original (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.overall_feedback: This paper offers an intriguing, historically grounded perspective on pattern recognition, successfully challenging the "deep learning is all you need" paradigm by demonstrating, through a specific application in Raman spectroscopy, that classic coincidence detection can achieve competitive results with significantly lower computational overhead. The writing is generally clear and motivated by a compelling synthesis of neuromorphic theory and practical utility. However, the manuscript suffers from a significant methodological gap: the transition from the biological/neuromorphic premise of coincidence detection to the practical implementation—a simple logistic regression—is opaque. The paper fails to sufficiently justify why a standard L2-regularized linear model serves as a valid or rigorous "approximation" of complex ion-channel dynamics and spatiotemporal coincidence detection. To improve the paper, the authors must bridge this theoretical disconnect, provide a more granular comparison of the signal processing architectures beyond raw accuracy (e.g., robustness to noise or data scarcity), and justify why the chosen dataset is a fair benchmark for evaluating the specific biological advantages of neuromorphic-inspired architectures.

comments.title: Missing Equation Definition
comments.quote:
50

51 where $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ is the zero-mean unit-variance normalization of $\mathbf{x}$.
comments.explanation: The text references 'the approximation in Eq. (1)' at line 52, but the equation itself is missing from the provided text between line 50 and 51, even though the placeholder '50' appears twice. While I understand this is likely an OCR or layout artifact where the LaTeX rendered object was lost, it is a major issue because the entire methodology of the paper rests on an 'approximation' for coincidence detection that is never provided for the reader to verify.

comments.title: Unexplained Operational Symbol
comments.quote: arg $n$ max( $\mathbf{x}$[] $\mathbf{w}_n$ ) where we introduce the operator[] to represent the coincidence between two signals.
comments.explanation: The operators are represented by the character '[]', which is clearly an OCR misread of a mathematical operator (likely a dot product, correlation, or custom operator). While the authors define it conceptually as 'coincidence,' the mathematical definition is crucial for reproducibility and for understanding the connection to normalized logistic regression mentioned in line 52. Without the precise definition of [], one cannot confirm if the 'approximation' in the missing Eq. 1 is mathematically sound.

comments.title: Inconsistent Claim about Data Split
comments.quote: they begin with a ResNet-26 pre-trained on $N \times$ 2000 spectra, then for each class $n = 1 : N$ there are $M = 100$ training spectra, and similarly $N \times M = 3000$ test spectra.
comments.explanation: The authors state $N = 30$ and $N \times M = 3000$. If there are 30 classes and $M=100$ training spectra per class, the math holds (30 * 100 = 3000). However, the confusion matrix at the end of the document shows 30 actual classes but the vertical/horizontal labels of the test set show values around 100 but also significantly higher/lower in some cases (e.g., class 17 has 144 entries). This discrepancy between the stated $M=100$ per class and the actual counts in the provided confusion matrix suggests either a misunderstanding of the dataset or a failure to follow the experimental protocol claimed in the text.

comments.title: Questionable Claim regarding Machine Learning Methodology
comments.quote: The coincidence detection (via normalized logistic regression) method introduced here achieves a test accuracy greater than ResNet-26
comments.explanation: The authors claim to outperform ResNet-26. However, they 'discard the pre-training data' used by the ResNet authors but then use the test set for their own evaluation. If the feature extraction of the ResNet model (which learned from the pre-training data) is fundamentally different from 'normalized logistic regression' on raw (or filtered) spectra, the comparison might be biased. Furthermore, the confusion matrix indicates highly unbalanced classes in the evaluation (ranging from 33 to 144 samples per class), yet the accuracy is reported as a single global percentage. Without explaining how they handled class imbalance (or if they calculated accuracy per class), the comparison to a standard ResNet baseline is underspecified.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The manuscript presents an intriguing perspective on classical neuromorphic principles but is significantly undermined by a lack of statistical rigor and a substantial disconnect between its ambitious framing and modest contributions. While the core finding—that a simple normalized logistic regression approach can marginally outperform a ResNet-26 on a specific Raman spectroscopy task—is notable, the reported accuracy gain (82.7% vs. 82.2%) is statistically insignificant without error bounds, variance estimates, or hypothesis testing, making the performance claim unconvincing. Moreover, the title and abstract promise a broad evaluation involving continual learning, neuromodulatory mechanisms, and spiking hardware, yet the work delivers only a static, batch-trained classifier, creating a misleading narrative that overstates both the novelty and the generalizability of the results. Critical technical flaws further detract from the paper's quality, most notably the absence of a defining equation for the proposed coincidence detection approximation in the Methods section, which obscures the algorithmic details, and the omission of inference latency or resource consumption metrics, which are essential to substantiate claims of efficiency beyond trivial training times. To be viable, the authors must provide rigorous statistical validation, supply the missing mathematical formulation, align the abstract with the actual scope of the experiments, and temper conclusions to reflect that this result demonstrates the sufficiency of a linear model for this particular dataset rather than a general replacement for deep learning architectures.

comments.title: Missing mathematical formulation and conceptual justification for the coincidence detection approximation
comments.quote:
approximation for the translation and scale-invariant property of coincidence detection as

- 50

- 51 where _**x**_ ˆ is the zero-mean unit-variance normalization of _**x**_ .
- 52 Thus, the approximation in Eq. (1) allows _y_ ( _**x**_ ) to be learnt by a logistic regression on the normalized dataset.
comments.explanation: The paper introduces a neuromorphic coincidence detection mechanism but abruptly transitions to referencing a missing Eq. (1) that maps this operation to static logistic regression. This equation is critical for understanding how the synaptic delays **w** are trained and why normalization suffices for the claimed invariance. Furthermore, while the work is motivated by temporal/spiking dynamics and neuromorphic efficiency, the implementation relies on a standard supervised classifier without justifying how the static linear approximation captures core neuromorphic benefits (e.g., energy efficiency, spike-timing robustness, or plasticity). The absence of the defining formula and the lack of bridge between the spiking motivation and the static ML implementation leave the core methodology underspecified, irreproducible, and conceptually misaligned.

comments.title: Ambiguous data partitioning and inconsistent statistical reporting in experimental results
comments.quote:
for each class _n_ = 1 : _N_ there are _M_ = 100 training spectra, and similarly _N × M_ = 3000 test spectra.

|82.2_±_0.3 (from [10])|82.7 (this work)|

Note that the training data was fit all at once to a 100% accuracy.
comments.explanation: Multiple experimental reporting gaps undermine reproducibility and statistical rigor. First, the dataset partitioning is ambiguously phrased ('_N × M_ = 3000 test spectra'), leaving the exact number of test samples per class and total split size unclear. Second, the proposed method is reported as a single point estimate (82.7%) while the baseline includes uncertainty (±0.3), and the checklist marks error bars as [N/A] without justification; this prevents assessing whether the observed improvement is statistically significant. Third, the paper highlights achieving 100% training accuracy as a notable result, which is trivial for high-dimensional linear models with weak regularization and lacks context regarding generalization performance. Together, these issues make it difficult to replicate the setup, compare fairly with baselines, or interpret the claimed performance gains.

progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive Original (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The manuscript presents an intriguing perspective on classical neuromorphic principles but is significantly undermined by a lack of statistical rigor and a substantial disconnect between its ambitious framing and modest contributions. While the core finding—that a simple normalized logistic regression approach can marginally outperform a ResNet-26 on a specific Raman spectroscopy task—is notable, the reported accuracy gain (82.7% vs. 82.2%) is statistically insignificant without error bounds, variance estimates, or hypothesis testing, making the performance claim unconvincing. Moreover, the title and abstract promise a broad evaluation involving continual learning, neuromodulatory mechanisms, and spiking hardware, yet the work delivers only a static, batch-trained classifier, creating a misleading narrative that overstates both the novelty and the generalizability of the results. Critical technical flaws further detract from the paper's quality, most notably the absence of a defining equation for the proposed coincidence detection approximation in the Methods section, which obscures the algorithmic details, and the omission of inference latency or resource consumption metrics, which are essential to substantiate claims of efficiency beyond trivial training times. To be viable, the authors must provide rigorous statistical validation, supply the missing mathematical formulation, align the abstract with the actual scope of the experiments, and temper conclusions to reflect that this result demonstrates the sufficiency of a linear model for this particular dataset rather than a general replacement for deep learning architectures.

comments.title: Missing mathematical formulation for the coincidence detection approximation
comments.quote:
approximation for the translation and scale-invariant property of coincidence detection as

- 50

- 51 where _**x**_ ˆ is the zero-mean unit-variance normalization of _**x**_ .
- 52 Thus, the approximation in Eq. (1) allows _y_ ( _**x**_ ) to be learnt by a logistic regression on the normalized 53 dataset.
comments.explanation: Initially, I was confused by the abrupt transition between describing the coincidence operator and immediately referencing 'Eq. (1)' to explain how the method maps to logistic regression. Upon reviewing the surrounding text, it becomes clear that an equation was entirely lost during PDF-to-text extraction, likely due to the presence of a mathematical symbol or layout that OCR failed to capture. The authors clearly intend to show how a neuromorphic coincidence operation can be reduced to a standard statistical learning problem. In neuromorphic computing, coincidence detection typically relies on precise temporal alignment of spikes, but here the authors appear to approximate it as a static, translation- and scale-invariant vector operation. Given the mention of zero-mean, unit-variance normalization and the subsequent use of logistic regression, the missing formula is almost certainly an inner product or cosine similarity between normalized input and class vectors. While I acknowledge the authors' creative conceptual bridge between classic spike-timing mechanisms and modern machine learning, the complete absence of the defining equation is a major gap. Without it, readers cannot verify how the coincidence operator [] is computed, how the synaptic delays **w** are trained, or why normalization alone suffices to capture the claimed invariance properties. This leaves the core methodology underspecified and irreproducible.

comments.title: Ambiguous description of training and test data splits
comments.quote: for each class _n_ = 1 : _N_ there are _M_ = 100 training spectra, and similarly _N × M_ = 3000 test spectra.
comments.explanation: At first glance, the dataset description seemed clear: 30 classes, 100 training spectra each, totaling 3000. However, the phrase 'and similarly _N × M_ = 3000 test spectra' created momentary doubt about whether the authors meant 100 test spectra per class (implying a 50/50 split) or if the entire dataset size was 3000 with an unspecified train/test partition. I checked the context and the referenced work [10], which typically uses balanced splits for spectral classification tasks, but the paper itself does not explicitly state the number of test samples per class or the total dataset size. In pattern recognition benchmarks, explicitly stating the partitioning strategy (e.g., '100 training and 100 testing spectra per class') is standard to prevent misinterpretation. While I recognize the authors' intent was likely to convey a balanced cross-validation or hold-out setup, the current phrasing leaves the exact evaluation configuration ambiguous. This could mislead a careful reader attempting to replicate the experimental setup or compare results against other studies that use different splits.

comments.title: Overstated significance of achieving 100% training accuracy
comments.quote: Note that the training data was fit all at once to a 100% accuracy.
comments.explanation: The authors explicitly call out achieving 100% training accuracy as a notable observation. While technically correct, my initial reaction was that this claim is trivial for the described setup. The method uses logistic regression with L2 regularization set to 0.0001 on a dataset of 3000 samples with 1000-dimensional input features. In standard machine learning practice, linear models with a feature-to-sample ratio this high (nearly 1:0.33) and weak regularization are routinely capable of perfectly memorizing training labels, especially when classes are well-separated in Raman spectroscopy space. I acknowledged that the authors might be highlighting this to emphasize the discriminative power of their normalized coincidence approximation, but in contemporary deep learning and statistical learning literature, reporting 100% training accuracy on an overparameterized linear model does not demonstrate model capacity or generalization strength without accompanying validation metrics. The statement is mildly overclaimed and risks misleading readers into equating training fit with methodological novelty or robustness. A brief qualification regarding expected memorization in this regime or a pivot to generalization performance would better align with standard ML reporting conventions.

comments.title: Missing variance for proposed method despite baseline reporting error bars
comments.quote:
|82.2_±_0.3 (from [10])|82.7 (this work)|

3. If you ran experiments...
(c) Did you report error bars (e.g., with respect to the random seed after running experiments multiple times)? [N/A]
comments.explanation: Initially, I noted that the ResNet-26 baseline in Table 1 reports accuracy with a stated uncertainty (±0.3), while the proposed coincidence detection method is listed as a single point estimate (82.7%). The checklist marks error bars as [N/A] without any justification. In empirical machine learning evaluations, when a baseline includes variance or confidence intervals, omitting uncertainty for the new method makes it impossible to determine whether the 0.5% improvement is statistically significant or simply falls within the noise margin of either approach. This methodological gap weakens the comparative claim that the proposed method outperforms the baseline and contradicts standard reproducibility norms for ML benchmarking.

comments.title: Conceptual gap between neuromorphic motivation and static logistic regression implementation
comments.quote: Thus, the approximation in Eq. (1) allows y(x) to be learnt by a logistic regression on the normalized dataset.
comments.explanation: The introduction and abstract frame the work around temporal/spiking coincidence detection, neuromorphic hardware, and continual learning mechanisms. However, the implemented method replaces the dynamic neuromorphic process with a static normalization step followed by a standard logistic regression classifier. While the authors acknowledge this as an 'approximation' (line 98), they do not justify how well this static linear model captures the core benefits of coincidence detection (e.g., energy efficiency, spike-timing robustness, or plasticity on neuromorphic substrates). The paper leaves a gap between its stated motivation (advanced neuromorphic learning) and its actual methodology (conventional supervised classification), which could mislead readers about the feasibility of the proposed neuromorphic advantages.

comments.title: Ambiguous appendix text block that could suggest a data contradiction
comments.explanation: This sequence of 30 integers appears in the appendix figure text extraction and sums exactly to 3000. If interpreted literally as the diagonal of a confusion matrix (correct predictions per class), they would imply 100% test accuracy, directly contradicting the stated 82.7%. However, given the known PDF-to-text OCR pipeline, these numbers strongly resemble tick labels from a heatmap's color scale axis, which are frequently mangled when figures are extracted as raw text. While I suspect an OCR artifact, a careful reader examining only this text excerpt could reasonably conclude a numerical inconsistency. The issue is resolvable by checking the actual PDF figure or the supplemental code, but it introduces unnecessary ambiguity.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper presents a commendable attempt to revisit classic neuromorphic principles, but it suffers from several critical flaws that undermine its central claim. Most fundamentally, the proposed “coincidence detection” method is merely logistic regression applied to normalized data—a far cry from the spiking, delay-based mechanisms of Rosenblatt or Hopfield. The paper offers no neuromorphic implementation, no spike-based encoding, and no exploitation of temporal dynamics; the claimed equivalence is not justified, and the operator \( [\cdot] \) introduced in Equation (1) remains undefined. Moreover, the experimental comparison is deeply problematic: the authors compare their single-run logistic regression accuracy (82.7%) against a reported ResNet-26 result (82.2%) from a different study, without re-implementing the deep learning baseline under identical conditions (e.g., same data splits, preprocessing, or evaluation protocol). The training set sizes and preprocessing steps appear to differ, and no statistical significance test is provided. The manuscript also lacks critical methodological details, such as the exact filter parameters, the learning algorithm’s convergence criteria, and the code for the “coincidence” operator. The writing is overly informal and hyperbolic (e.g., “cannot remain comfortably numb,” “All You Need”), which detracts from scientific objectivity. In summary, while the idea of revisiting classic methods is valuable, the paper does not convincingly demonstrate that coincidence detection—as properly defined—can compete with deep learning, and the current analysis is insufficient for publication at a top venue like NeurIPS.

comments.title: Missing formulation of coincidence detection approximation (Eq. (1)) and undefined operator
comments.quote:
We do not yet have a complete theory of neuromorphic signal processing, so here we introduce an approximation for the translation and scale-invariant property of 50 coincidence detection as

- 50

- 51 where **x**ˆ is the zero-mean unit-variance normalization of **x** .
comments.explanation: The core mathematical definition of the coincidence detection operator and its approximation are missing from the OCR text. The operator is represented as [] in `y(**x**) = arg _n_ max( **x**[] **w** _n_ )`, which is an unknown placeholder. The equation that should follow the word 'as' is absent, leaving the method underspecified and irreproducible. Context does not resolve this because the equation is simply not present. Without knowing the approximation, it is impossible to understand how logistic regression on normalized data relates to coincidence detection.

comments.title: Inconsistent usage of 'w' as both class representation and synaptic delays
comments.quote: With a more neuromorphic coincidence detection method and a learning method that adapts the synaptic delays **w** continually
comments.explanation: Earlier in Methods, `w_n` is defined as a vector representation for class n that is learnt via logistic regression. In the Results section, the same symbol `w` is referred to as 'synaptic delays', which is a different concept. Synaptic delays were introduced in the Introduction as transmission times that were randomly distributed and not learned. This inconsistency could mislead readers about what is being adapted and conflates learned weights with fixed delays.

comments.title: Comparison between models trained with different amounts of data
comments.quote: Here, we consider the work [10] of an interdisciplinary team, where a 26 layer convolutional neural network with residual connections (ResNet-26) was successfully trained for classifying pathogenic bacteria by Raman spectroscopy. In their work, there are _N_ = 30 classes of bacterial isolates and they begin with a ResNet-26 pre-trained on _N ×_ 2000 spectra, then for each class _n_ = 1 : _N_ there are _M_ = 100 training spectra... We discard the pre-training data
comments.explanation: The baseline ResNet-26 was pre-trained on 60,000 spectra (30 × 2000) before fine-tuning on 3,000 training spectra. The authors' coincidence detection method uses only the 3,000 training spectra and discards the pre-training data. Claiming that coincidence detection 'achieves a test accuracy greater than ResNet-26' is misleading because it compares a model with substantially more training data to one with less. A fair comparison would require training both methods on the same training set. This undermines the central claim of the paper.

comments.title: No error bars reported for coincidence detection accuracy
comments.quote: The coincidence detection (via normalized logistic regression) method introduced here achieves a test accuracy greater than ResNet-26 (see Table 1), and it took less than 3 seconds to train the classifier on a modern desktop
comments.explanation: Table 1 reports the ResNet-26 accuracy as 82.2 ± 0.3, but the coincidence detection accuracy is given as a single point (82.7%) without any variance estimate. The difference of 0.5% is within the error margin of the ResNet-26 result. Without repeated runs or confidence intervals, it is unclear whether the improvement is statistically significant. The checklist states 'N/A' for error bars, but for a comparison against a reported baseline with error bars, error bars on the new method are necessary.

comments.title: Underspecified preprocessing: 'range-1 mean filter'
comments.quote: pre-process the training and test spectra by a range-1 mean filter
comments.explanation: The description of the preprocessing step is ambiguous. A 'range-1 mean filter' could be interpreted as a moving average filter with window size 1 (which would leave the signal unchanged), or as a filter that subtracts the mean and divides by the range. Without a clear definition, another researcher cannot reproduce this step. Given that the overall method is already incompletely specified due to the missing equation, this adds further vagueness.

comments.title: Discrepancy between reported test accuracy and confusion matrix data
comments.quote: 82.7 (this work)
comments.explanation: Table 1 reports a test accuracy of 82.7% for coincidence detection. However, the confusion matrix data in the appendix appears to show counts per class that sum to 3000 (30 classes × 100 each), but the numbers in the picture text (e.g., '97 103 113 113 76 103 33 130 101 100...') are clearly not all 82.7% correct—for example, class 7 shows only 33, which is 33% accuracy. If the matrix represents test predictions, the overall accuracy would be much lower than 82.7%. Alternatively, if the matrix shows training data (which is stated to be 100% fit), the numbers '1 100', '2 100', etc. suggest perfect training accuracy, but the second matrix with varied numbers would then be test data and the average is not 82.7%. The values like 33, 144, 36, 143 seem inconsistent with an 82.7% overall accuracy (which would require ~2481 correct out of 3000, not the sum of the diagonal which appears much lower). This is a numerical inconsistency that undermines the main result.

progressive_original__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive Original (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive_original__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper presents a commendable attempt to revisit classic neuromorphic principles, but it suffers from several critical flaws that undermine its central claim. Most fundamentally, the proposed “coincidence detection” method is merely logistic regression applied to normalized data—a far cry from the spiking, delay-based mechanisms of Rosenblatt or Hopfield. The paper offers no neuromorphic implementation, no spike-based encoding, and no exploitation of temporal dynamics; the claimed equivalence is not justified, and the operator \( [\cdot] \) introduced in Equation (1) remains undefined. Moreover, the experimental comparison is deeply problematic: the authors compare their single-run logistic regression accuracy (82.7%) against a reported ResNet-26 result (82.2%) from a different study, without re-implementing the deep learning baseline under identical conditions (e.g., same data splits, preprocessing, or evaluation protocol). The training set sizes and preprocessing steps appear to differ, and no statistical significance test is provided. The manuscript also lacks critical methodological details, such as the exact filter parameters, the learning algorithm’s convergence criteria, and the code for the “coincidence” operator. The writing is overly informal and hyperbolic (e.g., “cannot remain comfortably numb,” “All You Need”), which detracts from scientific objectivity. In summary, while the idea of revisiting classic methods is valuable, the paper does not convincingly demonstrate that coincidence detection—as properly defined—can compete with deep learning, and the current analysis is insufficient for publication at a top venue like NeurIPS.

comments.title: Underspecified preprocessing: 'range-1 mean filter'

comments.explanation: The description of the preprocessing step is ambiguous. A 'range-1 mean filter' could be interpreted as a moving average filter with window size 1 (which would leave the signal unchanged), or as a filter that subtracts the mean and divides by the range. Without a clear definition, another researcher cannot reproduce this step. Given that the overall method is already incompletely specified due to the missing equation, this adds further vagueness.

comments.title: Missing equation reference and unclear approximation
comments.quote: Equation (1) makes it clear that we employ an approximation for coincidence detection.
comments.explanation: The checklist response claims that Equation (1) clarifies the approximation used, but in the passage provided, Equation (1) is incomplete—it appears only as a line number (50) with no formula shown. The earlier text (lines 49-51) mentions 'an approximation for the translation and scale-invariant property of coincidence detection as' and then just has a line number, suggesting the equation was lost in OCR. The reader cannot verify whether the approximation truly justifies the method or whether the claim in the checklist is accurate, because the equation is missing. This is an underspecification issue that affects reproducibility and justification.

comments.title: Discrepancy between reported test accuracy and confusion matrix data
comments.quote: 82.7 (this work)
comments.explanation: Table 1 reports a test accuracy of 82.7% for coincidence detection. However, the confusion matrix data in the appendix appears to show counts per class that sum to 3000 (30 classes × 100 each), but the numbers in the picture text (e.g., '97 103 113 113 76 103 33 130 101 100...') are clearly not all 82.7% correct—for example, class 7 shows only 33, which is 33% accuracy. If the matrix represents test predictions, the overall accuracy would be much lower than 82.7%. Alternatively, if the matrix shows training data (which is stated to be 100% fit), the numbers '1 100', '2 100', etc. suggest perfect training accuracy, but the second matrix with varied numbers would then be test data and the average is not 82.7%. The values like 33, 144, 36, 143 seem inconsistent with an 82.7% overall accuracy (which would require ~2481 correct out of 3000, not the sum of the diagonal which appears much lower). This is a numerical inconsistency that undermines the main result.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents a provocative claim that classic neuromorphic coincidence detection rivals modern deep learning, potentially challenging the prevailing complexity dogma in artificial intelligence. While the introductory historical context provides interesting background, the clarity is occasionally compromised by informal language and a somewhat idiosyncratic tone. The most significant issue, however, is a fundamental conceptual and methodological mismatch: the authors treat coincidence detection solely as a normalized dot product approximated by logistic regression (Eq. 1), effectively reducing the proposed method to standard linear classification without rigorous justification for why the "coincidence" mechanism is essential or how it transcends standard correlation-based approaches. Furthermore, the comparative evaluation lacks statistical rigor; the manuscript reports that performance is "greater than" a state-of-the-art baseline but omits specific accuracy values, confidence intervals, and details on the filter processing, making the central claim difficult to assess and validate.

comments.title: Inconsistency between stated test set size and reported confusion matrix sum
comments.quote:
Section 2: ... N x M = 3000 test spectra. 
Appendix Figure Text: ... [numerical list summing to approximately 2432]
comments.explanation: The paper explicitly defines the test dataset size as N x M = 3000 (where N=30 and M=100). However, the confusion matrix provided in the Appendix text (which corresponds to the test data) lists numerical entries that sum to approximately 2432 (excluding the training diagonal). This represents a discrepancy of nearly 568 samples (or ~19% of the stated total). While OCR artifacts are possible, the magnitude of this gap suggests either a significant error in the reported dataset size, an incomplete confusion matrix in the Appendix, or a fundamental mismatch between the method described in Section 2 and the results presented in Appendix A. This undermines the verification of the 82.7% accuracy claim, as the exact composition of the test set cannot be reconciled with the visual evidence.

progressive_original__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive Original (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive_original__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents a provocative claim that classic neuromorphic coincidence detection rivals modern deep learning, potentially challenging the prevailing complexity dogma in artificial intelligence. While the introductory historical context provides interesting background, the clarity is occasionally compromised by informal language and a somewhat idiosyncratic tone. The most significant issue, however, is a fundamental conceptual and methodological mismatch: the authors treat coincidence detection solely as a normalized dot product approximated by logistic regression (Eq. 1), effectively reducing the proposed method to standard linear classification without rigorous justification for why the "coincidence" mechanism is essential or how it transcends standard correlation-based approaches. Furthermore, the comparative evaluation lacks statistical rigor; the manuscript reports that performance is "greater than" a state-of-the-art baseline but omits specific accuracy values, confidence intervals, and details on the filter processing, making the central claim difficult to assess and validate.

comments.title: Inconsistency between stated test set size and reported confusion matrix sum
comments.quote:
Section 2: ... N x M = 3000 test spectra. 
Appendix Figure Text: ... [numerical list summing to approximately 2432]
comments.explanation: The paper explicitly defines the test dataset size as N x M = 3000 (where N=30 and M=100). However, the confusion matrix provided in the Appendix text (which corresponds to the test data) lists numerical entries that sum to approximately 2432 (excluding the training diagonal). This represents a discrepancy of nearly 568 samples (or ~19% of the stated total). While OCR artifacts are possible, the magnitude of this gap suggests either a significant error in the reported dataset size, an incomplete confusion matrix in the Appendix, or a fundamental mismatch between the method described in Section 2 and the results presented in Appendix A. This undermines the verification of the 82.7% accuracy claim, as the exact composition of the test set cannot be reconciled with the visual evidence.
